title: Writing - Part 1: Fast Fashion & Sustainability
description: Your class has listened to a lecture on the fast fashion industry and its implications for environmental and social sustainability.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the issues in your notes. You should explain which issue you consider most urgent, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Fast fashion creates enormous textile waste and toxic water pollution.
Garment workers in poor countries are paid poverty wages.
Cheap, disposable clothing encourages a throwaway mindset that undermines sustainability.

structure.introduction: Introduce the fast fashion debate and state which two issues your essay will examine.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first issue in depth, illustrating its scale and consequences.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second issue, then make a clear argument for which is the most urgent concern.
structure.conclusion: Briefly restate both issues and your overall judgement, offering a forward-looking perspective.

content.modelAnswer:
The fast fashion industry has transformed global clothing consumption, delivering low-cost garments at unprecedented speed. However, this model carries profound costs, with environmental pollution and exploitative labour practices emerging as its most serious consequences.

The environmental damage caused by fast fashion is staggering. The industry is responsible for approximately ten percent of global carbon emissions and is the second largest consumer of the world's water supply. Synthetic fibres shed microplastics into waterways, while mountains of discarded clothing pile up in landfills and informal dump sites across the developing world. The rapid turnover of trend cycles means that garments are often worn only a handful of times before being discarded, compounding waste at an alarming rate.

Equally troubling are the human costs embedded in fast fashion supply chains. Garment workers — the majority of whom are women in low-income countries — frequently endure poverty wages, unsafe working conditions, and the suppression of labour rights. The 2013 Rana Plaza disaster, in which over a thousand workers were killed in a factory collapse in Bangladesh, exposed the lethal consequences of prioritising profit over worker welfare. Despite public outcry, structural change within the industry has been slow.

Of the two issues, I consider exploitative labour practices the most urgent. Environmental damage, while catastrophic, operates on a longer timescale; workers are experiencing harm today, in real time, with their health and dignity at stake. Systemic reform of supply chain accountability must therefore be the immediate priority for policymakers and consumers alike.
